being, it's cool, right? So there's a book published in 1970 called The Physical Phenomena of Spiritualism by a guy named Karen. And in that book are the exact instructions for how to do the fire walk. Yourself or to rig it for others. And nobody gets burned. So it is at least as old a demo as 1907. I thought it was 30. Perhaps it goes back farther than that. But make no mistake, it's rigged. And nobody in their right mind is taking people who have briefly gone through a mental transformation in a seminar room and having them stroll across real hot coals, real hot coals barefoot. Nobody in their right mind. Because you'll be in court your whole life. That's where you're going to be. People assume McDonald's because they serve them hot coffee in a styrofoam cup that says hot coffee. And what do they do with it? Same stupid thing you do when you're in a car, does they have a cup holder? You put it between your legs while you drive the car. How many of you have done that? But you are not going to let a bunch of doofuses walk across. It's rigged. So there's a phone demo. I produced cassettes for a guy that used this. It's very common, by the way. Some version of this. His was pretty dramatic. So he's a telephone prospecting trainer, cold call guy. Right? Here's how to get appointments. And so insurance company would bring him in. Aluminum siding guys would bring him in. Doesn't matter. And here's his demo. So he would sit on stage with a phone, a real phone. Not a, not, not a, not a phone, a phone. And have a white pages telephone directory, Google it. And he either he or the client, the head of the insurance company, whatever would pick somebody at random out of the audience. And then they would have that person pick somebody else at random out of the audience. And they would come up on stage and they would take the white pages. There's no force, by the way, because the trick wasn't in the, in the directory. And they would pick three names and phone numbers out of the directory. And he, a phone hook to a speaker, he would cold call him and book appointments for whatever salesperson kind of, aluminum siding, wife, insurance, whatever. Right there on stage in front of everybody working without a net. And everybody would then stampede and buy the script book and the audio cassettes of the calls. It's a powerful demo. Of course, the calls are all pre-recorded. He's practiced to the point that he hits the empty spaces on the tape just right. The phone triggers the recording. If it's used twice in a row, it triggers the second recording. And if it's used three times in a row, it triggers the third recording. Only way you screw that up is if the tape jams, because this is in the days of cassettes, okay, happened once that I know of. But for the most part, this is flawless. Flawless. Get rich or real estate guys use the same thing. Now, the technology is a lot better. But you will see a get rich or real estate guy demo this actually making a deal on the phone. Call off his bow. Call somebody in foreclosure. You pick them at random out of this whole stack over here. I'll call them. And I'll get us to the point of a deal contingent upon inspection. No money down. Watch how easy it is. Well, they ain't calling the person you picked at random out of the stack. Not happening. They're either calling somebody in their own office or it's recorded. It's one or the other. The demo is rigged. Always. So, demos are like, what's the law rule? You never ask somebody a question you don't know the answer to. I'm telling you most of them are rigged. Most of them that you see are rigged. Sometimes a little, sometimes not so much, but they're rigged. And many times, though, they don't need to be. But the point is, if you can, you want a dramatic demonstration that somehow proves what it is that you do, or your product does, or your service does, in a memorable, sticky way. And whether you rig it or not is entirely up to you. And so it's the same straight jacket escape you would do on a stage, which his brother complained about. There's an episode of Boardwalk Empire where Hardin has performed, Houdini's brother, and he's done the straight jacket escape, and now he's up in the VIP suite with some people afterwards. And they're all talking about, not what he just did, but they're talking about Houdini doing the straight jacket escape, hanging upside down and dropping in the water off a dock, and every other way he does the straight jacket escape. And a rather testy and noid Hardin finally says, it's the same damn trick, which it is. In fact, Houdini says, it's easier to do it, hanging upside down from a crane, because I can't see what you're doing. If I do it on stage, you can see what I'm doing. If I'm 50 feet in the air, you can't see nothing, except I got out of it. Right? So it's actually, if you're not bothered by heights and hanging upside down, it's actually easier to do it up there. But there's a great demo for gel insoles that you put in your feet, and you're going to see two demos for these things. And the important thing to understand about those demos is they actually prove nothing about how this is going to work as a gel insole in your shoe worn all day. They prove nothing, zero. Yet in people's minds, they absolutely prove how fantastic these products are. So demos can be illogical. If you critically analyze them, they can be illogical. So since we're talking about gel insoles, Scholes figured this out. You'll see the TV ads, and you'll see the units in drug stores. So there's all these gel insoles now, but you go and you put your foot in a machine. And it customizes, so lights flash. It customizes the insole for you. The insoles are all the same, except for their length. So instead of selling the insole the old way, which if you ever bought these, you had lines on them, and you took a pair of scissors and you cut off the excess amount to fit it in your shoe. They now have more inventory because they're hung on a rack precut in sizes. The machine knows how long your foot is and tells you which insole to buy. The rest of it's all nonsense. It's not customizing anything. They all have the same amount of gel in them. It's the same insole. However, they're selling for 30% more because you're going in and using the machine with the flashing lights. This is not new. So women, guys have never seen this, probably. Okay, so process and device flips switches in people's head. And I'm going to be back a little bit after that. You're up. Was this an awesome morning or what? I mean, come on. Really unbelievable stuff. All right, raise your hand if you believe in ESP. Raise your hand if you believe in ESP. Okay, raise your hand if you can at least spell ESP. Okay, perfect. We're going to get along really great. So what I'm going to do is what's called mentalism. And it looks like psychic. It looks like I'm a mind reader. But what I'm actually doing is using my five senses to create the illusion of a sixth sense. So I use verbal communication, nonverbal communication, perceptual manipulation, and some downright trickery to accomplish this stuff. All right. So the first thing I'm going to do is I'm going to project a thought from my mind into your mind. Now to do this, everyone, put your feet flat on the floor. You don't need to take notes here. Feet flat on the floor. Take a deep breath. Okay, good. Perfect. And let it out slow. Excellent. All right. Now, the first thing we're going to project is a picture because our minds think in pictures. So for example, if I were to say the words, Dolly Parton to you, what are the first two things that pop into your mind? Right? Right? So we think in pictures. All right. So I want you to imagine that there's a movie screen up in front of you. And I'm going to send you two simple geometric symbols, one inside the other, like a square with something else inside. But obviously you don't think of a square because since I mentioned it, okay? So when I snap my fingers, think of two simple geometric symbols, one inside the other. Okay, by a show of hands, how many people got a triangle in a circle? Raise your hand. Raise your hand really high. Keep your hands up. How many of you got a triangle inside a circle? Keep your hands up. How many got the reverse of it? How many people got at least one of them? Right? Raise your hand. Look around the room. As Dan would say, look more around the room. Almost 100%. So how does that actually work? I'm going to teach you to so you can actually do it. All right? It is, as Dan called, a force. And it's forced in two ways. Right? So the first way it's forced is with my language. This is the verbal part of communication. This is a verbal part of persuasion. I said I want you to think of two simple, and I emphasized simple, geometric symbols. Right? Then I said like a square with something else inside, but obviously don't think of a square because I already mentioned it. Well, I've just eliminated the square. Right? So there's, now, you could think of a star. You could think of the only way to think of something like a trapezoid, though, if you weren't following instructions. Right? And so it was not a simple geometric symbol. So that's the verbal part. So I narrowed down what you can actually think of. Now, there's still a wide range. So how do I get almost 100% of the people to get it? Well, watch, you'll recall I did this. Let's replay. I want you to think of two simple geometric symbols. One inside the other. When I came out, my first thing I said to you, yeah, you can clap for that. That's good. It's pretty cool. Thank you. In fact, I did it right when I came out. I'm going to send a thought from my mind into your mind. All right? So the combination, right, the combination of the two things, verbal and nonverbal, you actually plan a thought in someone's mind.
